_description: Methodology template: Environmental Justice Screening. Pre-fills analysis section with evidence-based method stack for EJ index / pollution burden work at the census-tract level.

client.notes: Clarify whether this is for regulatory comment, grant support, community organizing, or internal planning. Framing differs significantly.

audience.role: Environmental planner, EJ advocate, or regulatory analyst
audience.what_they_are_deciding: Which communities to prioritize for pollution remediation, regulatory enforcement, or grant funding.
audience.what_they_already_believe: Communities of color and low-income communities bear a disproportionate pollution burden.
audience.political_or_institutional_context: EJ findings can be used in regulatory proceedings. Be precise about what the data can and cannot support legally.

engagement.hero_question: Which census tracts face the highest combined pollution burden and socioeconomic vulnerability, and how does that burden compare across demographic groups?
sensitive_findings_to_handle_carefully: Race and income correlation — present carefully, do not imply causation
Named industrial facilities — verify before publishing
Regulatory determination language — EJScreen is a screening tool, not a legal finding

known_spatial_issues: Superfund proximity is Euclidean — does not account for wind/water pathways
PM2.5 is modeled at ~1km resolution — sub-tract variation not captured
Industrial site addresses may have geocoding errors — validate against satellite imagery

primary_sources.name: EPA EJScreen
primary_sources.notes: Download tract-level CSV. Includes pm25_percentile, superfund_proximity, and composite EJ index columns. Use most recent vintage.

primary_sources.name: ACS 5-Year Estimates
primary_sources.notes: Derive poverty_rate and pct_minority (non-white or Hispanic per client definition).

primary_sources.name: EPA Superfund NPL Sites
primary_sources.notes: Point layer of National Priorities List sites. Used for proximity calculations and overlay.

primary_sources.name: EPA Facility Registry Service (FRS)
primary_sources.notes: Industrial facility locations for overlay. Filter by NAICS or permit type as appropriate.

primary_sources.name: TIGER/Line Tracts
primary_sources.notes: Use vintage matching ACS data year

known_data_quality_issues: EJScreen PM2.5 values are modeled from EPA monitors — not measured at the tract level
Superfund proximity does not distinguish active vs remediated sites
pct_minority definition varies — document the exact ACS fields used
institutional_areas_to_flag: Industrial zones may overlap residential tracts — check land-use context before flagging as residential exposure
Prisons inflate pct_minority in some tracts — consider flagging

analysis.dependent_variable_notes: Composite of pollution burden (pm25_percentile, superfund_proximity) weighted by population vulnerability (poverty_rate, pct_minority). Compute as: ej_index = (pm25_percentile + superfund_proximity_percentile) / 2 * (poverty_pct + minority_pct) / 2, or use EJScreen pre-computed column.

analysis_notes.bivariate_choropleth: Use analyze_bivariate.py with poverty_rate and pm25_percentile. 3×3 grid yields 9 classes. Highlight tracts in top-right cell (high poverty + high pollution) as priority areas.
analysis_notes.ej_index_choropleth: Use natural breaks with 5 classes. Consider percentile-based classification (quintiles) for comparability with EJScreen national rankings.
analysis_notes.hotspots: Run Getis-Ord Gi* on ej_index. Flag statistically significant hot spots (z > 1.96) for priority designation.
analysis_notes.industrial_overlay: Overlay FRS facility points on choropleth. Use size proportional to permitted emission tonnage if available. Annotate major facilities by name.
analysis_notes.regression_note: Hotspot and bivariate analysis are the primary deliverables. Regression is optional — if run, use OLS with robust SE given potential heteroskedasticity. Do not over-interpret causation.

report.pyramid_lead: [N] census tracts in [Study Area] rank in the top quintile for both pollution burden and socioeconomic vulnerability — placing an estimated [X] residents in the highest-priority environmental justice concern tier.

scqa.situation: [Study Area] includes [N] census tracts across [counties/jurisdictions]. The region hosts [X] industrial facilities and [Y] Superfund sites.
scqa.complication: EPA EJScreen data show [X]% of tracts in the top national quartile for PM2.5 burden, disproportionately overlapping with majority-minority, high-poverty communities.
scqa.question: Which specific tracts face the compounded burden of high pollution and high vulnerability, and what facilities drive exposure?
scqa.answer: [N] tracts meet the threshold for priority EJ designation under [applicable framework]. These tracts are home to [X] residents, [Y]% of whom are non-white, and [Z]% are below the poverty line.

_methodology_caveats: EJScreen is a screening tool, not a regulatory determination: EJScreen results identify communities that may warrant closer attention. They do not constitute a legal finding of disproportionate impact and should not be represented as such in regulatory filings without additional analysis.

Modeled vs measured data: PM2.5 values in EJScreen are modeled estimates interpolated from monitoring stations, not direct measurements. Spatial resolution is approximately 1km — sub-tract variation is not captured. Measured monitor data should supplement modeled values where available.

Superfund proximity is Euclidean: The proximity metric measures straight-line distance to the nearest NPL site, not actual exposure pathway. Wind direction, hydrology, and site remediation status are not reflected.

EJ index composite methodology: Document exactly how the composite index is constructed (EJScreen pre-computed vs custom). Different compositing methods yield different tract rankings — be explicit in the technical appendix.
Correlation is not causation: High overlap between pct_minority and pollution burden reflects systemic historical patterns. The analysis documents disparity; it does not establish legal causation.
